text: Two 5mm and two 12mm trocars are placed. The procedure begins with careful dissection on the medial side, identifying the right ureter. The dissection proceeds laterally, identifying and dissecting the left ureter from fibrosis. Adhesions on the anterior surface are carefully dissected. A gauze is placed over the iliac vessels for identification during medial dissection. The posterior rectum is completely dissected.

Two 5mm and two 12mm trocars are placed for laparoscopic access. The surgeon begins careful dissection on the medial side, identifying the right ureter and incorporating it into the descent along the medial side.

The surgeon continues dissection on the lateral side, identifying and dissecting the left ureter from surrounding fibrosis. Adhesions on the anterior surface are carefully dissected. A gauze is placed over the iliac vessels for identification during medial dissection. The posterior aspect of the rectum is completely dissected.

A polypropylene mesh is fixed to the promontory using spiral tackles, and secured to the anterior and medial surface of the rectum with non-absorbable sutures. The surgeon identifies prolapse of the anterior pelvic structures and performs colpopexy using the same technique. The peritoneum is closed to prevent direct contact between intra-abdominal organs and the polypropylene mesh.
The surgeon fixes a polypropylene mesh to the sacral promontory using spiral tackles. The mesh is then secured to the anterior and medial surface of the rectum with non-absorbable sutures.
The surgeon identifies prolapse of the anterior pelvic structures and performs colpopexy. The peritoneum is closed to prevent direct contact between intra-abdominal organs and the polypropylene mesh.
